sentences.text: My name is Stan Rubens.
S-T-A-N, and the last name, R-U-B-- for boy-- E-N-S.
I was born November 16, 1932.
That was in the city of Enschede in Holland.
E-N-S-C-H-E-D-E.
It's in the Netherlands.
Enschede is located very close to the German border, on the eastern part in the middle of the Netherlands.
I am, at the moment, 64.
Well, I guess one of the first memories I, I have from when my father had a store, a retail store, in, in the city of Enschede, and I stood on top of the stairs, which was a double stairs.
And I-- there was a little gate there for me not to go down the stairs.
Of course, I managed to open the gate and fell promptly down the stairs.
That's the only memory I have from Enschede.
And thereafter, we moved to Amsterdam.
I don't remember the year, but-- '32, '34, '35, '36, '37-- I think it must have been about '37 or '38, 1937 or '38.
No, I never went to school in Enschede.
Because I was four years old or four and 1/2 years old when I left Enschede.
Didn't go to kindergarten there.
That I did in-- when I was in Amsterdam.
My father, his name was Jacob Paul Rubens.
He was in-- how to call that?
I guess best explained as he had a retail store in glassware, chinaware, crystal, leather goods, custom jewelry, something like that.
Yeah, the store had a name.
It was called Rubens [DUTCH], which translated to mean Rubens, the House of Gifts.
I think he was-- actually, I think my father was a troubled person, quite troubled with many things.
He and I, we had very little communications.
If I would ask him something like, how does this work or what about this?
He would say, well, you're a little bit, little bit too young for that.
I'll tell you that when you grow up-- when you grow up more.
So we did not have much communication.
He was reasonably kind, I would think, though he was very quick with his hands to slap.
And I guess I, I grew up in an atmosphere of uncertainty and a lot of tension because of my father's attitude towards the family.
And who was your mother?
My mother is Eva Themans.
And do you want me to spell that?
T-H-E-M-A-N-S.
And she was born in Enschede, and where my parents met and lived for a while till they moved to Amsterdam.
I think my father felt that it was a economic better situation for him with-- to have a store in Amsterdam than to have a store in Enschede.
So it was for economic reasons that he did that.
And my, my, mother, yeah-- how do I describe my mother?
Both my parents were always working.
So I didn't see that much of them.
Later on, when I look back at them, I have a feeling that they were-- how they found each other is, to me, a riddle.
Because each one of them was usually very selfish towards their own, own objectives and what they were doing.
I think they had financial difficulties to, to cope and deal with making life as good as possible for themselves.
I think that was the main thing.
My mother's-- my relationship with my mother and I was fine.
We had no difficulties.
And that-- the reason why, in retrospect, I, I, I feel sometimes I was kind of being played in between.
Because my father would not hesitate to hit my mother.
And I rebelled in the strongest fashion against that and literally, would jump in between the two of them and stop them from fighting.
So the result was that my mother, of course, was very kind to me.
And my father sometimes was ready to kick me in the [NON-ENGLISH] Because I, I was interfering with his macho position.
My, my mother worked with my father in the store.
When I was a little kid, my-- I had a governess, I guess you would call it, who would come in at about 9 o'clock and take me to kindergarten.
And we were staying at a pension, where the food was ready when my parents came home.
And they had a couple of rooms there for my sister, who was a few years older than I am.
And we lived there for-- 'til about 1939, for a short time.
That was in Amsterdam.
That was on the-- what is now called the [?
Churchillaan. ?] It had another name before.
I can't remember that name at that time.
And thereafter, we moved to the Apollolaan, number 47, where we had-- were at a ground floor.
And that's, that's where we moved.
By then, I was about six years old.
And my, my parents thought it was quite all right, when I was six years old, for me to walk to school, which was about a, maybe a mile and a half.
And the [NON-ENGLISH] from the Apollolaan, and they would go out and leave me in charge of the house.
So I guess, basically, I became very independent at a very early age.
Yeah.
Her name is Ronny.
Basically, we cared very much for each other.
It was a, it was, and is still, a very good relationship, by which we are always very concerned for each other's well being.
If I describe her, I would say that she is-- she was-- when she was very young, she was extremely, extremely impatient.
She was so impatient, there are no words to describe that.
Everything-- she had a very volatile character.
What's that lady who was married to-- who speaks with a very terrible Mexican accent [INAUDIBLE].
Oh, come on.
She plays here in Hawaii also-- who comes across as a very emotional, volatile person.
And that was my, and that was and is my sister.
She's quieted down maybe just a little bit, but very, very emotional, very emotional, and still is, basically speaking.
No.
We were not religious at all.
We maybe went to synagogue once a year for Yom Kippur.
I was very conscious of the fact that I was Jewish.
And when I was, oh, about six to seven years old, I even went to Sunday school for a very short time, until the war broke out and this became totally impossible.
Right.
If they did, I was too young to understand it.
Because I was only six years old at that time.
I went to school at [INAUDIBLE] in Amsterdam, first and second year.
But in the second year, I went to another school which was closer to, closer to the house where I was living.
And by then, I think the war had already broken out.
It was 1940 by then.
So 19-- early-- late 1940 or-- yeah, I think in September of 1940, I went to that other school already, to my second grade preliminary school or elementary school.
Yes.
No, no.
That was somewhere else.
That was [INAUDIBLE], Let me give that in English.
[NON-ENGLISH] 106, 108.
As a matter of fact, yes-- usually after school, I would go to the, to the store, because my father wanted me to work in the store.
As young as I was, he had always things for me to do.
That I do not recall at all.
There was a fair amount of Jewish people, of course, in those days living in Amsterdam.
There was a big, a big Jewish community in Amsterdam, very big.
Of course, most of them disappeared during the Nazi occupation and, and, and the aftermath of that and the result of that.
No, that would be difficult for me to do because of my-- I was only six, seven years old, so--
What impressed me was the, the Yiddish speaking, which, strangely enough, I didn't really care that much for.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was totally unaware of-- you see, my, my parents never paid much attention to me.
And maybe it was that when I was in that area, that they paid a lot more attention to children than my parents paid to me.
And it almost felt embarrassing to me.
Maybe that was the reason.
There was much more kindness, actually, in the ghetto, though it wasn't a closed off ghetto like in other cities.
It was a total open area, but the area where a lot of Jewish people would congregate.
And I think the only reason why we came there, because on occasion, maybe we wanted some kosher meat.
Maybe that's the only reason how I got there.
I still can totally picture, as young as I was at the time, the butchery shop where we would buy some meat.
There's not much to tell.
It was just one open window, and an open space with a counter, and the butchers-- two or three butchers-- would be standing behind it.
Very simple, very simple-- some butcher blocks and that was it.
Again, very difficult for me to realize how poor they were, or if they were poor.
Everybody seemed well.
And everybody seemed in a reasonable good mood.
But the typical Jewishness, I was not used to it.
And because of this typical Jewishness, the, the, the dialects, the, the very Jewish words that were being spoken, the Yiddish that was being spoken, it was not an, an, an area of comfort for me.
I didn't feel that comfortable with it.
And strangely enough, if I use-- sometimes when I talk to my daughter and I use a Yiddish expression, she will look at me and say, oh, come on, Dad.
Knock it off.
She doesn't like it, either.
And I immediately translate that when I was in her shoes or at her age or whatever, I didn't like it either.
And somehow, this seems to have carried through in my daughter also.
She's not interested in the, the typical Jewish expressions or the, the, the Yiddish things, even though I sometimes-- I've learned over the years, of course, a fair amount of Yiddish words.
But I, I don't use them.
I use them very sparingly.
Of the ghetto?
Maybe there was an electricity in the air.
I am very sensitive to, to, to the way a, a room-- I can walk into a house, into a room, into an area.
Any area I walk into, I pick up vibrations.
That's the word I was looking for.
And the moment I pick up those vibrations, they get translated into good, bad, evil, medium, danger, watch out.
I get all this in-- just in a flash.
It comes to me whether this is right or wrong.

And it has helped me in my business in later years, where I, I can walk into a, into an establishment and immediately sense if this place is doing well, doing bad, mediocre, if there has been a fight, if there has been arguments, and things like that.
No, I really did not.
And I'll, I'll remember when, when my grandmother-- because my grandmother on my mother's side was very liberal.
And my grandmother on my father's side was not so liberal.
So when I went to my grandmother for vacation and I ate some butter or milk with meat and I said, am I allowed to, to do that?
No, they were not Jewish schools.
Yes, as a matter of fact, I, I-- well, not so much when the war broke out, but when the Germans came into, into Amsterdam.

And I, I still see myself, ridiculous as it may seem, as a little boy standing at the corner in front of the Apollo Hall-- at that time, I think they still have ice skating there-- and, and the corner of the Apollolaan.
And I happen to-- just happen to be there.
I was always roaming around, going places.
Nothing seemed to scare me, really, to go anywhere, as a little kid even.
And I, I was standing at the corner.
And the German occupation came into, into Amsterdam.
And some of the guys were waving to me.
And I kind of waved back.
And then almost like my other hand wanted to grab my hand down, don't do that.
These are not good people you want to wave to.
But I, I will never forget that particular scene, how I was waving to the Germans and saying to myself, I'm nuts.
I shouldn't be waving to, to them.
That's the enemy.
Yeah, that's, that's, that's how I remember the first Germans coming to, to Amsterdam.
Never did, never did-- the only thing was that I think children pick up the underlying fear from their parents immediately.
A little child will starts-- will start to cry when a mother starts to cry.
It seems to transfer itself automatically.
I think children, of course, are very sensitive to, also to vibrations in the air.
So in, in this case, I was picking up the vibrations in the air, and said, oh boy.
We are in deep trouble.
And then my father started to explain to me that we may have to go into hiding or underground from the, from the Nazis.
Because they were-- and he said, when I was in Germany, and he had lived for many years in Germany, and he spoke fluent German.
He spoke so good German that, German that he could pass for German, as a matter of fact.
He, he said, well, he said, this is going the wrong way.
We are going to be in, in big difficulties.
And like, he bought a lot of food when the war broke out, like a lot of people did in Amsterdam, or all over, to cover themselves in case of shortages.
I still don't care that much for tomato soup with rice because of the hundreds of cans of tomato soup and rice we had.
But I, I felt thoroughly, completely, that this was difficult times.
And I knew we were at war.
I think it was somewhere around about October of '41, or maybe September.
It was still very warm.
And my father-- it was a warm time.
Must have been September.
My father said, OK, he said, put on about three, four times the underwear that you normally wear and as much clothing as you can without looking bulky.
Because you can't take a suitcase with you.
And we, we going to go to another place.
But we're just going to walk there like hey, nothing is happening.
He had a car.
And-- but the car was taken away from him.
He had a store, but a [INAUDIBLE], as they called it, somebody walked into the store and said to him, hey, [INAUDIBLE], Jew, Rubens, get out.
I am taking over your store.
And he had barely a chance to grab his coat and his hat to, to get out of the store without being literally kicked out of the store.
No, I absolutely do not remember that, because my--
It was a Dutch person.
Yeah, it was a Dutch person.
You know, there were a lot of good Dutch people.
But also, there were quite a bit of bad Dutch people in the-- during the war time.
You know all, all about that, of course.
Well, as you may know, the Germans were very subtle about their prosecution event.
It reminds me of the story of the frog that is being boiled in a kettle by a Frenchman.
And the, the water is, is kind of cool.
And then the Frenchman builds his fire underneath the pot.
And the frog is in the pot.
And he says, jeez, actually, I'm, I'm comfortable.
Water is getting a little bit warmer, but I am OK.
And as the water heats up and heats up, all of a sudden, bit by bit, the frog realizes the water has gotten so hot that he can't jump out of the pot anymore.
And this is the approach that the Germans did.
They just built up the heat bit by bit by bit.
First, everybody had to report.
Then, it became necessary for everybody to wear a Jewish star.
Everybody who was Jewish had to report.
And they had to-- needed an Ausweis, a passport or identification papers.
And the identification papers would show that you were a Jew.
Because it had a J stamped on it.
And then later on, you, you had to wear the yellow star.
And then later on, all of a sudden--

Oh yes, very distinctly, very distinctly-- because I remember in later years, and I'm talking living in Hawaii when I was in my-- maybe, like 10 years ago, I was the technical assistant to the play of And one of the girls-- and I'm moving way ahead now to give you an explanation on that yellow star-- one of the girls that was doing the detailing for the dress, for the dressing and how to dress the people, walked up to me.
And she said, I copied this from an, from an information book.
And she laid in front of me the yellow star I had worn.
It was like somebody, all of a sudden, hit me with a two-by-four in the face.
And I literally went-- yes.
Yes, yes.
I, I remember.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
And I wanted to be professional about this.
And I wanted to keep my emotion out of this whole thing and caught myself.
But the first reaction-- I hadn't seen anything like that since, since the war.
And when I saw this, it totally, it completely threw me, threw me back physically almost a couple of steps.
I did a couple of steps backwards to, to absorb this, this impact of this Jewish star that I had worn.
And maybe later on, I can explain to you why it meant so much to me, because I was wearing it for many-- for, for quite a while.
Anyhow, we'll come to that later.
Not particularly, except that my mother did a good job in sewing stars on different outer garments that I would be wearing.
In general, there was no reactions.
There, there was no reactions in general.
Nobody ever said anything to me.
I-- my, my character is very accepting and phlegmatic to a certain degree.
And I felt, OK, if that's what I have to do.
As a little kid, what are you going to say?
So I said, well we have to wear this.
So, all right, now, I just accepted it without any, any argument.
Because I knew that I could not argue about the matter.
Yes, I had non-Jewish friends.
Actually, at that age, I never looked at friends whether they were Jewish or non-Jewish.
It was totally unimportant for me at that time.

OK, there were situations where I was being-- I remember there was one situation where some kids were kind of, yeah, yeah, yeah, you're a Jew, you're a Jew, you're-- and things like that, were hollering at me.
And then one day-- and I told my father about that.
And my father said, well, he said, I want to know more about that.
And for the first time in his life-- and I was so proud of him-- he stood up for me.
Because I kind of pulled them along close to where my father was.
And then my father got out and tried to beat one of the kids up.
But the kids were way too fast for him, so he couldn't catch any.
After that, I never had any more troubles of anybody trying to jeer at me about being Jewish.
No, I didn't.
My, my father did that.
Because I, I was a, a six-year-old kid at that time-- six, seven.
No.
As a matter of fact, my mother was on my father's papers also.
My mother didn't have her own papers.
Yeah.
It was just a time of tension.
And you were always looking over your shoulders.
You, you didn't know what the Germans were going to do next.
That, that was the whole thing about this.
And so we, we were becoming fearful.
And the, the level of fear, the magnitude of fear, was starting to build up in everybody's life.
That, that was the point that was taking place.
The, the, the fear started to penetrate us.
And because of the fear, we went underground.
We went upstairs.
And it was in the evening.
And my father said, OK, let's, let's go to bed.
And we went to bed.
I took off most of my clothes because it was getting very hot.
And that is it.
And we stayed there for, oh, about a couple of months-- no, about five or six weeks, and didn't do anything.
We just sat around.
And then my-- finally, my mother and my sister decided to join us.
Because we heard rumors all over the place that people were being taken away by the Nazis and to places.
